content.text: A conversational journey map documents how a user moves through a multi-turn AI interaction, turn by turn, from the first message to the final outcome. Unlike a standard customer journey map, it must model AI-specific phenomena: intent ambiguity, slot filling, confidence-gated behaviour, and graceful degradation paths. Done well, it becomes the shared source of truth for product managers, conversation designers, and engineers building the same system.

attrs.alt: Conversational Journey Map — Customer Support Chatbot

content.text: Why Conversational Journey Maps Differ from Standard Journey Maps
Standard journey maps track touchpoints and emotions across a linear experience. Conversational AI adds three complicating factors:
Non-linear paths.
A user can correct a slot, abandon and resume, or switch intent mid-conversation. The map must model branching states, not just a left-to-right timeline.
Invisible AI decisions.

The AI makes classification decisions the user never sees — intent confidence, entity resolution, safety checks. These decisions produce user-visible effects (an unexpected question, a refusal, a slow response) that the map must capture in the System Actions row.
Error recovery as a first-class phase.
Standard journey maps treat errors as exceptions. In conversational AI, misunderstanding recovery is a recurring phase, not an edge case. It needs its own column in the map.
Map Structure and Rows
A conversational journey map uses columns for journey phases and rows for dimensions. The five standard rows are:
Row
What It Captures
GenAI Extension
User Action
What the user types or says
Include correction and abandonment actions, not just forward progress
AI Behaviour
What the conversational AI does and says
Include confidence-gated responses: when does the AI ask vs. act?
Emotion
User's emotional state at each phase
Frustration peaks during slot-filling; recovery depends on confirmation UX quality
Pain Points
Where the experience breaks down
AI-specific: wrong intent class, over-asking, hallucinated facts, rigid slot order
Opportunities
Design improvements the map reveals
AI-specific: batch slot extraction, confidence-gated clarification, persistent memory
System Actions
Backend events and state transitions
Dialog state writes, intent events, memory reads, safety classifier calls
Identifying Journey Phases
For most task-oriented conversational flows, the phases follow a common pattern. Adjust the names to match your domain but keep the logical sequence.

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from enum import Enum

class JourneyPhase(str, Enum):
    ENTRY           = "entry"            # user opens; AI greets and probes intent
    INTENT_RESOLVE  = "intent_resolve"   # intent classified; clarified if ambiguous
    SLOT_FILLING    = "slot_filling"     # required entities collected turn by turn
    CONFIRMATION    = "confirmation"     # collected data shown; user approves or edits
    RESOLUTION      = "resolution"       # AI executes action; result shown
    EXIT            = "exit"             # session ends; rating collected; memory saved

# Recovery phases (inserted between any two standard phases)
class RecoveryPhase(str, Enum):
    CORRECTION   = "correction"   # user fixes a previously filled slot
    ESCALATION   = "escalation"   # AI unable to proceed; transfers to human
    ABANDONMENT  = "abandonment"  # user stops responding; session times out
Modelling AI Behaviour Across Phases
The AI Behaviour row requires more detail than a human agent behaviour row because the decision logic is programmable and must be made explicit before implementation begins.

from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Optional

@dataclass
class AIBehaviourSpec:
    """One cell in the AI Behaviour row of the journey map."""
    phase:            JourneyPhase
    primary_action:   str           # what the AI says or does
    confidence_gate:  Optional[float] = None  # if P < gate: deviate from primary
    low_confidence_action: Optional[str] = None  # fallback when gate triggers
    max_turns:        int  = 1      # turns allowed in this phase before escalation
    emits_events:     list[str] = None  # system events triggered

    def __post_init__(self):
        if self.emits_events is None:
            self.emits_events = []

    def should_deviate(self, confidence: float) -> bool:
        if self.confidence_gate is None:
            return False
        return confidence < self.confidence_gate

SUPPORT_BOT_BEHAVIOURS = [
    AIBehaviourSpec(
        phase=JourneyPhase.ENTRY,
        primary_action="Greet user; load prior session context; send open probe",
        emits_events=["session_start", "memory_load"],
    ),
    AIBehaviourSpec(
        phase=JourneyPhase.INTENT_RESOLVE,
        primary_action="Classify intent and proceed to slot filling",
        confidence_gate=0.85,
        low_confidence_action="Ask single clarifying question targeting top-2 intent candidates",
        max_turns=2,
        emits_events=["intent_classified"],
    ),
    AIBehaviourSpec(
        phase=JourneyPhase.SLOT_FILLING,
        primary_action="Extract slots from user message; ask for missing required slots one at a time",
        max_turns=6,
        emits_events=["slot_filled", "entity_validated"],
    ),
    AIBehaviourSpec(
        phase=JourneyPhase.CONFIRMATION,
        primary_action="Render slot summary card; await user confirmation or correction",
        max_turns=3,
        emits_events=["confirmation_requested", "slot_corrected"],
    ),
    AIBehaviourSpec(
        phase=JourneyPhase.RESOLUTION,
        primary_action="Execute backend action; display outcome with source citation",
        confidence_gate=0.70,
        low_confidence_action="Offer human escalation with conversation summary",
        max_turns=1,
        emits_events=["action_executed", "resolution_shown"],
    ),
]
Mapping Pain Points to Design Signals
Pain points in a conversational journey map have a direct path to design decisions. The table below shows the most common AI-specific pain points and their recommended interventions.
Phase
Pain Point
Root Cause
Design Intervention
Entry
Impersonal greeting with no context
No persistent memory
Load last-session context on open; pre-populate known slots
Intent Resolution
AI asks a question user already answered
Confidence gate too aggressive or not reading prior turns
Batch-extract slots from opening message before classifying intent
Slot Filling
Too many back-and-forth turns for simple requests
One-slot-per-turn policy regardless of message richness
Extract all available slots from a single long message; ask only for genuinely missing slots
Slot Filling
Rigid order: AI always asks for slot A before B
Hard-coded slot collection sequence
Allow any order; mark slots filled as received, not by position
Confirmation
Slot summary hard to read in text
Plain-text summary of structured data
Render slot cards with inline edit affordances
Resolution
AI states a fact confidently but wrong
No uncertainty signalling or citation
Require source citation for factual claims; surface confidence score in UI
Resolution
No route to human for complex cases
Escalation not designed as a first-class path
Add confidence-gated escalation offer after N unresolved turns
Capturing the Emotional Arc

The emotional arc is a line through the Emotion row that shows how user sentiment evolves. In conversational AI, frustration typically builds during slot-filling (many back-and-forth turns) and recovers sharply at confirmation (finally seen and understood). This pattern has a concrete implication: the confirmation UX is disproportionately important for overall session satisfaction.

from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Callable

@dataclass
class EmotionDataPoint:
    phase:   JourneyPhase
    valence: float      # -1.0 (very negative) to +1.0 (very positive)
    label:   str        # human-readable label for the map

# Typical pattern for a task-completion chatbot
SUPPORT_BOT_EMOTIONS: list[EmotionDataPoint] = [
    EmotionDataPoint(JourneyPhase.ENTRY,          0.0,  "Neutral / hurried"),
    EmotionDataPoint(JourneyPhase.INTENT_RESOLVE, -0.2, "Mild frustration"),
    EmotionDataPoint(JourneyPhase.SLOT_FILLING,   -0.5, "Impatient (repeating)"),
    EmotionDataPoint(JourneyPhase.CONFIRMATION,    0.3, "Relieved (heard)"),
    EmotionDataPoint(JourneyPhase.RESOLUTION,      0.7, "Satisfied"),
    EmotionDataPoint(JourneyPhase.EXIT,            0.6, "Done"),
]

def frustration_peak(points: list[EmotionDataPoint]) -> EmotionDataPoint:
    return min(points, key=lambda p: p.valence)

def recovery_gradient(points: list[EmotionDataPoint]) -> float:
    """Slope from frustration peak to next positive point."""
    peak_idx = points.index(frustration_peak(points))
    if peak_idx + 1 >= len(points):
        return 0.0
    return points[peak_idx + 1].valence - points[peak_idx].valence

The recovery gradient from slot-filling to confirmation is the most actionable number the emotional arc produces. A gradient below 0.5 points means confirmation UX is not providing enough relief — redesign the slot summary card before optimising any other phase.
Multi-Path Journey Maps
A single journey map describes one representative path. A complete map also documents the most common deviations and how the system handles them.

from dataclasses import dataclass, field

@dataclass
class JourneyVariant:
    variant_id:   str
    trigger:      str          # what causes this variant
    affected_phases: list[JourneyPhase]
    ai_behaviour: str          # what the AI does differently
    emotion_delta: float       # change in valence vs. happy path at trigger point
    design_note:  str = ""

SUPPORT_BOT_VARIANTS = [
    JourneyVariant(
        variant_id="V01-CORRECTION",
        trigger="User says 'actually, my order number is different'",
        affected_phases=[JourneyPhase.SLOT_FILLING, JourneyPhase.CONFIRMATION],
        ai_behaviour="Reset only the corrected slot; preserve all other slots; return to confirmation",
        emotion_delta=-0.2,
        design_note="Correction should not restart the entire flow",
    ),
    JourneyVariant(
        variant_id="V02-ABANDONMENT",
        trigger="No user input for 5 minutes mid-slot-filling",
        affected_phases=[JourneyPhase.SLOT_FILLING, JourneyPhase.EXIT],
        ai_behaviour="Send one re-engagement message; if still no response, save state and close session",
        emotion_delta=-0.3,
        design_note="Saved state allows resume without re-entering slots",
    ),
    JourneyVariant(
        variant_id="V03-ESCALATION",
        trigger="Resolution confidence below threshold after 2 attempts",
        affected_phases=[JourneyPhase.RESOLUTION, JourneyPhase.EXIT],
        ai_behaviour="Offer human agent handoff with pre-populated conversation summary",
        emotion_delta=0.1,  # slight improvement: user feels not abandoned
        design_note="Summary must include all slots and the unresolved question",
    ),
    JourneyVariant(
        variant_id="V04-SCOPE-SWITCH",
        trigger="User asks an out-of-scope question mid-flow",
        affected_phases=[JourneyPhase.SLOT_FILLING],
        ai_behaviour="Acknowledge the question; offer to answer after completing current task OR restart",
        emotion_delta=-0.1,
        design_note="Do not silently ignore the scope-switch — acknowledge it explicitly",
    ),
]
From Journey Map to Artefact Handoffs
The journey map produces three downstream artefacts consumed by different team members.
Artefact
Derived From
Consumed By
Format
Dialogue flow spec
AI Behaviour row + variants
Conversation designer
Node graph with intent / slot / condition edges
System event catalogue
System Actions row
Backend engineer
Event schema table with payload and trigger phase
Eval scenario seeds
Pain Points + Emotion row
QA / ML engineer
Job stories per phase, typed by emotion and failure mode
UI component spec
Opportunities row
Frontend engineer
Component list: slot cards, confidence badges, correction affordances

from dataclasses import dataclass, field

@dataclass
class JourneyMapHandoff:
    """Summary of artefacts produced from a conversational journey map."""
    map_id:              str
    dialogue_flow_nodes: list[str]      # IDs of dialogue nodes derived from AI Behaviour row
    system_events:       list[str]      # event names from System Actions row
    eval_scenario_ids:   list[str]      # scenario IDs seeded from Pain Points row
    ui_components:       list[str]      # components from Opportunities row
    open_questions:      list[str]      # unresolved design decisions from map session

    def readiness_check(self) -> dict:
        return {
            "dialogue_flow_complete": len(self.dialogue_flow_nodes) > 0,
            "events_catalogued":      len(self.system_events) > 0,
            "eval_seeds_generated":   len(self.eval_scenario_ids) > 0,
            "ui_spec_started":        len(self.ui_components) > 0,
            "open_questions_resolved": len(self.open_questions) == 0,
        }
Do not move to implementation until
open_questions

is empty. Journey map sessions commonly surface 3–7 unresolved questions about memory scope, escalation ownership, or confidence threshold values. Resolving them during mapping is significantly cheaper than mid-sprint.
Running a Conversational Journey Mapping Workshop

Journey maps built by one person reflect one perspective. A workshop format surfaces disagreements early, aligns engineers on constraints, and builds shared ownership of the conversation design decisions.
Workshop Participants and Roles
Role
Contribution to the Map
Common Blind Spot Without Them
Product manager
Journey scope, business goals, success metrics
Map optimises for user delight but ignores conversion or CSAT target
Conversation designer
AI Behaviour row, variant branches, tone and repair strategies
Behaviour row becomes a wish list with no NLU feasibility check
ML / NLP engineer
Confidence threshold values, slot extraction constraints, latency budgets
Map assumes perfect intent classification; ignores P < 0.85 paths
Frontend / UX designer
Emotion row, pain points, UI components from Opportunities row
Pain points diagnosed but no actionable component design output
QA / eval engineer
Translates Pain Points into eval scenario seeds
Journey map produces no testing artefacts
Domain expert (optional)
Validates factual accuracy of resolution examples
Resolution phase assumes the AI can answer questions it cannot
Workshop Agenda (half-day format)
Block
Duration
Activity
Output
30 min
Scope: define start / end points, task type, primary persona
Agreed journey scope card
45 min
Phase identification: list phases on sticky notes; cluster and name
Phase list with definitions
60 min
Row fill-in: work through User Action and AI Behaviour together
Two rows completed
30 min
Emotion row + pain points: each participant annotates silently, then discuss
Emotion arc and consolidated pain point list
30 min
Opportunities and System Actions: derive from pain points and behaviour
Opportunities + event list
15 min
Variant identification: name the top 3 off-happy-path branches
Variant list with triggers
30 min
Open questions: capture and assign owners; agree resolve-by date
Open question log with owners

Allocate the full 30 minutes to block 7 — open questions. Teams that skip it leave the workshop with apparent alignment but surface the unresolved decisions as last-minute engineering debates during sprint 1.
Journey Mapping for Agentic Flows

Agentic AI flows — where the model plans and executes sequences of actions autonomously — require extensions to the standard conversational map. The AI is no longer just responding; it is deciding what to do next, which tools to call, and when to check back with the user.

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from enum import Enum

class AgentPhase(str, Enum):
    USER_GOAL     = "user_goal"      # user states a high-level objective
    PLAN          = "plan"           # agent produces a plan (may show to user)
    TOOL_CALL     = "tool_call"      # agent calls one or more external tools
    VERIFY        = "verify"         # agent checks result before showing user
    HUMAN_CONFIRM = "human_confirm"  # agent pauses for human approval (high-stakes)
    DELIVER       = "deliver"        # agent delivers final result
    REFLECT       = "reflect"        # agent reports what it did and why

@dataclass
class AgentBehaviourSpec:
    phase:               AgentPhase
    autonomy_level:      str    # "full" | "supervised" | "gated"
    visible_to_user:     bool   # does the user see this phase?
    max_tool_calls:      int = 0
    escalation_trigger:  str = ""  # condition that pauses for human review

AGENTIC_SUPPORT_FLOW = [
    AgentBehaviourSpec(
        phase=AgentPhase.USER_GOAL,
        autonomy_level="supervised",
        visible_to_user=True,
    ),
    AgentBehaviourSpec(
        phase=AgentPhase.PLAN,
        autonomy_level="supervised",
        visible_to_user=True,     # show plan; user can abort before execution
        escalation_trigger="plan involves irreversible actions (delete, send, charge)",
    ),
    AgentBehaviourSpec(
        phase=AgentPhase.TOOL_CALL,
        autonomy_level="full",
        visible_to_user=False,    # tool calls run in background
        max_tool_calls=5,
        escalation_trigger="tool error rate > 2 in sequence",
    ),
    AgentBehaviourSpec(
        phase=AgentPhase.HUMAN_CONFIRM,
        autonomy_level="gated",
        visible_to_user=True,     # explicit approval required
    ),
    AgentBehaviourSpec(
        phase=AgentPhase.DELIVER,
        autonomy_level="supervised",
        visible_to_user=True,
    ),
]
Key differences from a task-bot map:
Plan visibility.
Agentic flows should show the plan before execution, not just the result. The Plan phase is a checkpoint in the journey, not a hidden implementation detail.
Tool calls are invisible phases.
Users experience the gap between request and result as latency. The map must include a progress indicator or interim update in the Tool Call row.
Irreversibility gates.
Any action that cannot be undone (sending an email, charging a card, deleting a file) must trigger a Human Confirm phase. This is a non-negotiable journey stage, not an optional UX affordance.
Reflect phase.
After delivery, agentic flows benefit from a brief explanation of what actions were taken. This builds appropriate trust and allows users to catch errors before consequences propagate.
Measuring Journey Map Quality Over Time
A journey map is a living document. Measure its accuracy against real usage data and update it when the data diverges from the map's predictions.
Map Prediction
Measurement Signal
Tool / Query
Frustration peak at slot-filling
CSAT drop in slot-filling turns
Segment CSAT by session.turn_phase == 'slot_filling'
Recovery at confirmation
CSAT recovery rate after confirmation turn
Compare CSAT pre/post confirmation event in session logs
Correction variant triggers ≤ 15% of sessions
Correction event rate
Count sessions with slot_corrected event / total sessions
Escalation rate ≤ 5% post-resolution
Escalation event rate after resolution
Count escalation_triggered events / resolution_shown events
Max turns in slot-filling ≤ 4
P95 turn count in slot_filling phase
Percentile of turns_in_phase for slot_filling per session
Audit the journey map against production KPIs every sprint. When more than two KPIs drift beyond threshold in the same phase, that phase needs a re-mapping session, not a quick patch.
Summary

Conversational journey maps extend the standard journey-map format with AI-specific rows and phases. The five key extensions are: confidence-gated AI behaviour in the Behaviour row, frustration accumulation during slot-filling in the Emotion row, AI-specific pain points (intent errors, rigid slot order, hallucination) in the Pain Points row, system events in a dedicated System Actions row, and explicit variant branches for correction, abandonment, escalation, and scope-switch.
Model the AI Behaviour row with confidence gates — the threshold values become configurable parameters, not hard-coded constants.
The slot-filling to confirmation gradient is the most actionable metric in the emotional arc: low gradient → redesign the confirmation UX before any other phase.
Document journey variants (correction, abandonment, escalation, scope-switch) as first-class paths, not edge cases.
The journey map produces four downstream artefacts: dialogue flow spec, system event catalogue, eval scenario seeds, and UI component spec. All four must exist before sprint kickoff.
Resolve open questions surfaced during mapping before implementation — they are the cheapest point in the lifecycle to make design decisions.

A well-constructed conversational journey map prevents the most common conversational AI design failures: over-asking, invisible confidence failures, and recovery experiences that abandon the user at the moment of greatest frustration.
